title: Post-call recap + bench ask: hyperscaler gap-proof benchmarking
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Emily Park <emily.park@skylinehealth.com>, Jason Liu <jason.liu@hypercloud.ai>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-11-07T15:20:00-08:00
Subject: Post-call recap + bench ask: hyperscaler gap-proof benchmarking

Thanks Emily/Jason — good call today. Quick written recap and the minimal ask so we can produce an apples-to-apples bench readout next week.

Summary (what we covered)
- Skyline is evaluating end-user latency and cost tradeoffs between running inference on Hyperscaler-managed endpoints and Redwood Dedicated.
- Priority signals: p95/p99 tail latency for 1–8 concurrent users, steady-state tokens/sec, and cold/warm start behavior for short prompts (~40–120 tokens).
- You want a short, reproducible bench run we can share with graphs and a CSV of raw samples to present to your infrastructure committee.

What Redwood will deliver
- A/B run plan that compares: Hyperscaler endpoint (your managed instance) vs Redwood Dedicated pool (configured with identical model variant, request payloads, and concurrency profile).
- Measurements: p50/p95/p99 latency, tokens/sec (sustained at target QPS), throughput curves for concurrency sweep, and memory/KV cache hit notes.
- Both cold-start and warm-run captures (we'll seed KV cache for warm runs).
- Raw results CSV + an analyst-friendly summary (PNG charts + short one-page takeaway).

What we need from you to start
1) Representative prompt set (10–20 prompts) — can be synthetic but must reflect your user mix (transactional vs long-form). PDF or plain CSV is fine.
2) Guidance on the Hyperscaler endpoint to use (URL + expected config) and a point contact at your infra team who can allow the short load test (10–15 minutes each bucket).
3) Target concurrency buckets to test (we propose 1, 4, 8, 16 concurrency).

Attached: gapproof-bench-plan.csv (includes kickoff matrix), results-template.xlsx for how we'll report samples, and a placeholder p95_p99-warm-cold.png to be updated with results.

Proposed timing
- Start as early as Monday 2027-11-08 and deliver a first-pass results pack by Wednesday 2027-11-10. If that works, we can schedule a 30-min readout with your infra & procurement.

Next steps (simple)
- Emily: share prompt set + hyperscaler endpoint contact
- Jason: confirm available test windows for your endpoint team (10–15 minutes per bucket)
- Monica/Logan: we'll stand up the Redwood run and capture telemetry + raw CSV.

Thanks — Ben
Ben Carter | Enterprise Sales | Redwood Inference
T: (415) 555-0137 | ben.carter@redwood.ai


From: Emily Park <emily.park@skylinehealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Jason Liu <jason.liu@hypercloud.ai>
Date: 2027-11-07T16:05:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Post-call recap + bench ask: hyperscaler gap-proof benchmarking

Thanks Ben — clear summary and thanks for pulling the plan together. A few clarifying asks from our infra team before we schedule the window:

1) Can you give an example of the p95/p99 deltas you expect to be able to measure? We need to know the likely magnitude (order of ms) to set expectations with our SREs.
2) We need both payload sizes: small (40 tokens) and medium (120 tokens). Please include both in the run matrix.
3) Hyperscaler endpoint: we'll provide an endpoint URL but the team needs to see the exact traffic pattern. We prefer the A/B to be traffic-neutral (no stealth load) — can you do a client-driven comparison where Redwood calls both endpoints from a single runner?

Timeline: we can share the prompt set by EOD Mon (11/08). We can confirm endpoint access and SRE window for Tue morning.

If you can include an explicit bench plan with expected results range (e.g., p95 difference likely ~50-250ms depending on model/config), that will speed approvals.

—Emily
Emily Park | Director of Platform Engineering | Skyline Health


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Emily Park <emily.park@skylinehealth.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-11-07T17:12:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Post-call recap + bench ask: hyperscaler gap-proof benchmarking

Emily — thanks. Quick, concrete ranges and the exact A/B approach we recommend. Also looping in Monica / Logan who will run the tests.

Expected guardrail ranges (based on recent similar runs):
- Small payload (~40 tokens): expected p95 gap between Hyperscaler-managed endpoint and Redwood Dedicated: 40–180 ms (Redwood lower in most configs when prefetched/KV caching enabled). p99 gap: 80–300 ms.
- Medium payload (~120 tokens): expected p95 gap: 80–300 ms. p99 gap: 150–550 ms.
- Tokens/sec (sustained): on a 1-GPU equivalent serving pool, steady-state throughput for this model class typically lands between ~1,000–2,200 tokens/sec depending on batching strategy and quantization; Redwood's continuous-batching config aims to trade a small p50 increase for higher tokens/sec.

A/B methodology (client-driven single-runner approach)
- Single runner alternates traffic buckets to Hyperscaler endpoint and Redwood endpoint in 60s windows so both endpoints see identical prompt sequence and timing. This keeps load visible to your SREs and avoids cross-account interference.
- For warm runs we'll pre-warm with 100 requests to seed KV / caches; cold runs will start from an idle runner after a 30+ minute quiet period.
- Concurrency sweep: 1, 4, 8, 16. Each bucket executes 500 requests per endpoint per payload size (small/medium).
- Measurement export: per-request latency, server-side trace id (if available), tokens generated, and a column for vCPU/GPU instance type used on the Hyperscaler side.

Engineering ask (who does what)
- Monica: stand up Redwood runner + perf instrumentation; export CSV and charts.
- Logan: coordinate with your SRE (Emily) on the client-runner and share the test harness (we'll provide a lightweight open-source runner).

We will attach a concrete bench matrix (attached gapproof-bench-plan.csv) and populate the results-template.xlsx during the run.

If this looks good, Emily — please send the prompt CSV and the Hyperscaler endpoint contact and we’ll lock a Tue morning slot.

Best — Ben
Ben Carter | Enterprise Sales | Redwood Inference


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.ai>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood.ai>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-11-09T10:05:00-08:00
Subject: INTERNAL: Run checklist & owners for Skyline gap-proof bench (Tue run)

Internal owners — final checklist before we run Tue 11/09 08:00 PT (Emily confirmed). Please treat this as the run SOP and mark done in thread.

Priyom: infra + autoscaling knobs
- Confirm Redwood Dedicated pool on n1-standard-32 equivalent (2x A100-equivalent gpus) — ensure we pin model version and quantize to int8 per bench plan.
- Enable continuous batching with max batch size 48 and 60ms batching window.
- Export per-request traces (trace id + timestamps) to the shared S3 bucket.

Logan: runner + reproducibility
- Share the runner repo link and client-runner config with Emily by EOD Mon. Use the alternation windows (60s) described in the plan.
- Pre-warm sequence for warm runs: run 100 boot requests per model variant.

Monica: reporting + QA

Notes: keep total run time under 90 minutes to reduce disruption to Skyline infra. Prioritize the small payload runs first (they show tail behavior more clearly).

I'll follow up in the thread with S3 paths and the runner link once Priyom confirms infra.

text:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Emily Park <emily.park@skylinehealth.com>, Jason Liu <jason.liu@hypercloud.ai>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-11-07T15:20:00-08:00
Subject: Post-call recap + bench ask: hyperscaler gap-proof benchmarking

Thanks Emily/Jason — good call today. Quick written recap and the minimal ask so we can produce an apples-to-apples bench readout next week.

Summary (what we covered)
- Skyline is evaluating end-user latency and cost tradeoffs between running inference on Hyperscaler-managed endpoints and Redwood Dedicated.
- Priority signals: p95/p99 tail latency for 1–8 concurrent users, steady-state tokens/sec, and cold/warm start behavior for short prompts (~40–120 tokens).
- You want a short, reproducible bench run we can share with graphs and a CSV of raw samples to present to your infrastructure committee.

What Redwood will deliver
- A/B run plan that compares: Hyperscaler endpoint (your managed instance) vs Redwood Dedicated pool (configured with identical model variant, request payloads, and concurrency profile).
- Measurements: p50/p95/p99 latency, tokens/sec (sustained at target QPS), throughput curves for concurrency sweep, and memory/KV cache hit notes.
- Both cold-start and warm-run captures (we'll seed KV cache for warm runs).
- Raw results CSV + an analyst-friendly summary (PNG charts + short one-page takeaway).

What we need from you to start
1) Representative prompt set (10–20 prompts) — can be synthetic but must reflect your user mix (transactional vs long-form). PDF or plain CSV is fine.
2) Guidance on the Hyperscaler endpoint to use (URL + expected config) and a point contact at your infra team who can allow the short load test (10–15 minutes each bucket).
3) Target concurrency buckets to test (we propose 1, 4, 8, 16 concurrency).

Attached: gapproof-bench-plan.csv (includes kickoff matrix), results-template.xlsx for how we'll report samples, and a placeholder p95_p99-warm-cold.png to be updated with results.

Proposed timing
- Start as early as Monday 2027-11-08 and deliver a first-pass results pack by Wednesday 2027-11-10. If that works, we can schedule a 30-min readout with your infra & procurement.

Next steps (simple)
- Emily: share prompt set + hyperscaler endpoint contact
- Jason: confirm available test windows for your endpoint team (10–15 minutes per bucket)
- Monica/Logan: we'll stand up the Redwood run and capture telemetry + raw CSV.

Thanks — Ben
Ben Carter | Enterprise Sales | Redwood Inference
T: (415) 555-0137 | ben.carter@redwood.ai


From: Emily Park <emily.park@skylinehealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Jason Liu <jason.liu@hypercloud.ai>
Date: 2027-11-07T16:05:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Post-call recap + bench ask: hyperscaler gap-proof benchmarking

Thanks Ben — clear summary and thanks for pulling the plan together. A few clarifying asks from our infra team before we schedule the window:

1) Can you give an example of the p95/p99 deltas you expect to be able to measure? We need to know the likely magnitude (order of ms) to set expectations with our SREs.
2) We need both payload sizes: small (40 tokens) and medium (120 tokens). Please include both in the run matrix.
3) Hyperscaler endpoint: we'll provide an endpoint URL but the team needs to see the exact traffic pattern. We prefer the A/B to be traffic-neutral (no stealth load) — can you do a client-driven comparison where Redwood calls both endpoints from a single runner?

Timeline: we can share the prompt set by EOD Mon (11/08). We can confirm endpoint access and SRE window for Tue morning.

If you can include an explicit bench plan with expected results range (e.g., p95 difference likely ~50-250ms depending on model/config), that will speed approvals.

—Emily
Emily Park | Director of Platform Engineering | Skyline Health


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Emily Park <emily.park@skylinehealth.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-11-07T17:12:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Post-call recap + bench ask: hyperscaler gap-proof benchmarking

Emily — thanks. Quick, concrete ranges and the exact A/B approach we recommend. Also looping in Monica / Logan who will run the tests.

Expected guardrail ranges (based on recent similar runs):
- Small payload (~40 tokens): expected p95 gap between Hyperscaler-managed endpoint and Redwood Dedicated: 40–180 ms (Redwood lower in most configs when prefetched/KV caching enabled). p99 gap: 80–300 ms.
- Medium payload (~120 tokens): expected p95 gap: 80–300 ms. p99 gap: 150–550 ms.
- Tokens/sec (sustained): on a 1-GPU equivalent serving pool, steady-state throughput for this model class typically lands between ~1,000–2,200 tokens/sec depending on batching strategy and quantization; Redwood's continuous-batching config aims to trade a small p50 increase for higher tokens/sec.

A/B methodology (client-driven single-runner approach)
- Single runner alternates traffic buckets to Hyperscaler endpoint and Redwood endpoint in 60s windows so both endpoints see identical prompt sequence and timing. This keeps load visible to your SREs and avoids cross-account interference.
- For warm runs we'll pre-warm with 100 requests to seed KV / caches; cold runs will start from an idle runner after a 30+ minute quiet period.
- Concurrency sweep: 1, 4, 8, 16. Each bucket executes 500 requests per endpoint per payload size (small/medium).
- Measurement export: per-request latency, server-side trace id (if available), tokens generated, and a column for vCPU/GPU instance type used on the Hyperscaler side.

Engineering ask (who does what)
- Monica: stand up Redwood runner + perf instrumentation; export CSV and charts.
- Logan: coordinate with your SRE (Emily) on the client-runner and share the test harness (we'll provide a lightweight open-source runner).

We will attach a concrete bench matrix (attached gapproof-bench-plan.csv) and populate the results-template.xlsx during the run.

If this looks good, Emily — please send the prompt CSV and the Hyperscaler endpoint contact and we’ll lock a Tue morning slot.

Best — Ben

title: Acceptance & invoicing cadence for HealthSync — onboarding bump + invoice tranches
preview:
From: Tom Ellis <tom.ellis@healthsync.com>
To: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>, Ruth Patel <ruth.patel@healthsync.com>
Date: 2027-10-02T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Acceptance & invoicing cadence for HealthSync — initial ask

Hi Avery —

Thanks for the walkthrough yesterday. Our procurement team asked for a clearer acceptance schedule and an option to split the initial annual PO into tranches so our CFO can smooth cash flow while we ramp usage. We also want a short-term capacity bump for the first 60 days (approx +30% committed GPU hours) to ensure benchmarks on our integration tests.

Can you confirm:
- Whether Redwood can accept a single PO split into three invoices (50/30/20) across 90 days?
- If we can provision the extra capacity immediately upon PO signature for the 60-day ramp?
- What paperwork you need from us (SOC2 copy, line of business attestation, billing contact)?

Attaching a draft PO (stamped internal).

Attachments:
- HealthSync_PO_draft.pdf (PDF, 122KB)

Appreciate a quick confirmation — Ruth (CFO) is on the thread.

Thanks,
Tom
Head of Ops, HealthSync



From: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.com>
To: Tom Ellis <tom.ellis@healthsync.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-10-02T11:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Acceptance & invoicing cadence for HealthSync — onboarding bump + invoice tranches

Tom —

Appreciate the clear ask. Short answers up front: yes on tranches (we can accept a single PO and invoice in multiple tranches), and yes we can provision the capacity bump on signature with a brief canary period. Below is the recommended, low-friction path we use for a configuration like this.

Proposed plan (high level):
1) PO & invoices: Customer signs a single PO referencing deal HS-2027-Q4-UPSELL-1427. Redwood issues three invoices on milestone schedule: 50% at acceptance, 30% at day 30, 20% at day 90. We can add explicit language for invoice dates to match HealthSync's fiscal calendar.
2) Capacity bump: On DocuSign signature we provision the +30% reserved capacity to a new "onboarding" pool for 60 days. After 60 days the pool scales down automatically to the committed baseline unless you ask to continue.
3) Acceptance window: We propose a 14-day technical acceptance window with a two-step signoff: (a) integration smoke tests (internal), (b) production canary verification (ops). If acceptance passes, final acceptance invoice is released.
4) Evidence & compliance: Please attach SOC2 Type II report and the billing contact; security team will send a short attestation checklist for data residency and retention.

Attachments I added for ops/legal: redwood_onboarding-runbook.xlsx (contains milestones, gating criteria, support SLAs).

Next steps:
- I will have Legal draft the invoice schedule clause and add a DocuSign flow for the PO.
- Kimberly, can you slot in the invoice milestone language and an acceptance clause?
- Marissa, can you confirm on the tax/PO remittance name for the 50% invoice?
- Tom/Ruth, if quarterly invoicing is a hard constraint, let us know and we’ll adjust cadence.

If that works I’ll kick off the DocuSign envelope today and schedule a brief ops handoff.

Best,
Avery Johnson
Account Lead — Redwood Inference



From: Ruth Patel <ruth.patel@healthsync.com>
To: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.com>, Tom Ellis <tom.ellis@healthsync.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-10-03T08:27:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Acceptance & invoicing cadence for HealthSync — invoicing preference

On Fri, Oct 2, 2027 at 11:03 AM Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.com> wrote:
> Tom —
> 
> Appreciate the clear ask. Short answers up front: yes on tranches...

Avery / Tom —

Thanks for the quick turnaround. Our preference is to keep billing quarterly if possible (one invoice per quarter) to match internal reporting. For the first 90 days we can accept three equally spaced invoices if necessary, but quarterly is strongly preferred.

Also: our security team will need SOC2 Type II and the onboarding runbook (we need to confirm data retention windows for logs). Can you provide the attestation checklist you mentioned?

I can route the PO once the invoice cadence is clarified.

— Ruth
CFO, HealthSync



From: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.com>
To: Ruth Patel <ruth.patel@healthsync.com>, Tom Ellis <tom.ellis@healthsync.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-10-05T15:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Acceptance & invoicing cadence for HealthSync — final operational plan

Ruth / Tom —

Thanks — we can accommodate quarterly invoicing and still do the 60-day onboarding bump. Slight change to avoid accounting mismatches: we'll accept the single PO and split the first year's charge into two invoices for Q4 and Q1 (50/50), with the onboarding bump cost captured as a short-term prorated credit/charge in the Q4 invoice and a reconciliation line in Q1. This keeps your CFO's quarterly view intact while giving the ops team immediate capacity.

Operational responsibilities:
- Redwood (Avery / Marissa): Draft invoice clause and DocuSign envelope, add a reconciliation line item for the onboarding bump. (Target: send DocuSign by 2027-10-06 12:00 PT).
- Finance (Kimberly): Confirm remittance name, VAT handling, and if we need a separate W-9/declaring entity.
- Customer (Tom / Ruth): Confirm the billing contact and the preferred invoice PO number format.
- Security (Logan): I’ll loop Logan for the attestation checklist. Logan — SOC2 Type II and data retention windows are the asks; can you send the checklist and expected turn-around?

Links & attachments:

From: Tom Ellis <tom.ellis@healthsync.com>
To: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>, Ruth Patel <ruth.patel@healthsync.com>
Date: 2027-10-02T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Acceptance & invoicing cadence for HealthSync — initial ask

Hi Avery —

Thanks for the walkthrough yesterday. Our procurement team asked for a clearer acceptance schedule and an option to split the initial annual PO into tranches so our CFO can smooth cash flow while we ramp usage. We also want a short-term capacity bump for the first 60 days (approx +30% committed GPU hours) to ensure benchmarks on our integration tests.

Can you confirm:
- Whether Redwood can accept a single PO split into three invoices (50/30/20) across 90 days?
- If we can provision the extra capacity immediately upon PO signature for the 60-day ramp?
- What paperwork you need from us (SOC2 copy, line of business attestation, billing contact)?

Attaching a draft PO (stamped internal).

Attachments:
- HealthSync_PO_draft.pdf (PDF, 122KB)

Appreciate a quick confirmation — Ruth (CFO) is on the thread.

Thanks,
Tom
Head of Ops, HealthSync



From: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.com>
To: Tom Ellis <tom.ellis@healthsync.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-10-02T11:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Acceptance & invoicing cadence for HealthSync — onboarding bump + invoice tranches

Tom —

Appreciate the clear ask. Short answers up front: yes on tranches (we can accept a single PO and invoice in multiple tranches), and yes we can provision the capacity bump on signature with a brief canary period. Below is the recommended, low-friction path we use for a configuration like this.

Proposed plan (high level):
1) PO & invoices: Customer signs a single PO referencing deal HS-2027-Q4-UPSELL-1427. Redwood issues three invoices on milestone schedule: 50% at acceptance, 30% at day 30, 20% at day 90. We can add explicit language for invoice dates to match HealthSync's fiscal calendar.
2) Capacity bump: On DocuSign signature we provision the +30% reserved capacity to a new "onboarding" pool for 60 days. After 60 days the pool scales down automatically to the committed baseline unless you ask to continue.
3) Acceptance window: We propose a 14-day technical acceptance window with a two-step signoff: (a) integration smoke tests (internal), (b) production canary verification (ops). If acceptance passes, final acceptance invoice is released.
4) Evidence & compliance: Please attach SOC2 Type II report and the billing contact; security team will send a short attestation checklist for data residency and retention.

Attachments I added for ops/legal: redwood_onboarding-runbook.xlsx (contains milestones, gating criteria, support SLAs).

Next steps:
- I will have Legal draft the invoice schedule clause and add a DocuSign flow for the PO.
- Kimberly, can you slot in the invoice milestone language and an acceptance clause?
- Marissa, can you confirm on the tax/PO remittance name for the 50% invoice?
- Tom/Ruth, if quarterly invoicing is a hard constraint, let us know and we’ll adjust cadence.

If that works I’ll kick off the DocuSign envelope today and schedule a brief ops handoff.

Best,
Avery Johnson
Account Lead — Redwood Inference



From: Ruth Patel <ruth.patel@healthsync.com>
To: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.com>, Tom Ellis <tom.ellis@healthsync.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-10-03T08:27:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Acceptance & invoicing cadence for HealthSync — invoicing preference

On Fri, Oct 2, 2027 at 11:03 AM Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.com> wrote:
> Tom —
> 
> Appreciate the clear ask. Short answers up front: yes on tranches...

Avery / Tom —

Thanks for the quick turnaround. Our preference is to keep billing quarterly if possible (one invoice per quarter) to match internal reporting. For the first 90 days we can accept three equally spaced invoices if necessary, but quarterly is strongly preferred.

Also: our security team will need SOC2 Type II and the onboarding runbook (we need to confirm data retention windows for logs). Can you provide the attestation checklist you mentioned?

I can route the PO once the invoice cadence is clarified.

— Ruth
CFO, HealthSync

Thanks — we can accommodate quarterly invoicing and still do the 60-day onboarding bump. Slight change to avoid accounting mismatches: we'll accept the single PO and split the first year's charge into two invoices for Q4 and Q1 (50/50), with the onboarding bump cost captured as a short-term prorated credit/charge in the Q4 invoice and a reconciliation line in Q1. This keeps your CFO's quarterly view intact while giving the ops team immediate capacity.

Operational responsibilities:

- Finance (Kimberly): Confirm remittance name, VAT handling, and if we need a separate W-9/declaring entity.
- Customer (Tom / Ruth): Confirm the billing contact and the preferred invoice PO number format.
- Security (Logan): I’ll loop Logan for the attestation checklist. Logan — SOC2 Type II and data retention windows are the asks; can you send the checklist and expected turn-around?

Subject: Portal activation: contract redlines & signature / invoicing clause

Hi Evelyn,

Thanks for the quick intro last week. Attached is our annotated MSA — the legal team inserted a couple of invoicing and signature-related clauses that will impact how invoices flow through your portal (ProcureCo requires PO number on all invoices, and our AP team needs a remit-to address and GL code mapping).

Two quick questions before we schedule a joint redline walkthrough with your legal/ops teams:
- Who will be the signatory on Redwood's side for the Order Form? (we need name, title, and signer email for DocuSign)
- Can your portal accept invoices that arrive without a PO but reference a service order id? Our AP team wants clarity on failure/reject behavior.

Elena Rivera
Head of Strategic Sourcing
ProcureCo
elena@procureco.com
(415) 555-0148

Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-09-07T10:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Portal activation: contract redlines & signature / invoicing clause

Hi Elena — thanks for the redline. Looping in Legal (Marissa), Ops (Logan) and Finance (Kim) so we can triage.

Quick responses up front:
- Signer: Redwood typically uses the Head of Legal for MSAs; we can use our VP of Partnerships for the Order Form if you need a business signatory. I will confirm exact name/email right after this note.
- Portal behavior: Redwood's portal will accept invoices without a legacy PO only if the invoice includes the matched service order id and our PO-accept rule is set to "match-by-order-id". That said, if ProcureCo insists on PO-only acceptance, we can flip to strict PO validation but that will cause automated rejections for invoices currently submitted without PO numbers.

Marissa — legal asks to highlight three items for the redline walkthrough: limitation of liability carveouts, indemnity reciprocal language, and the data residency paragraph (ProcureCo flagged an EU data residency request).

Logan — ops needs a short field mapping doc from ProcureCo showing what they send as GL/Cost Center and whether they will include an EDI 810 (invoice) or post via portal upload.

Kim — finance, can you confirm whether ProcureCo's payment terms (Net 60) and remit-to align with our accounting setup? If they need an alternate remit-to, we'll need tax/1099 info on file.

Proposed next step: 30-min redline walkthrough tomorrow at 09:00 PT. Elena — does that work? If yes I'll set a calendar invite and include a DocuSign powerform link to capture signatory details.

Thanks,
Evelyn Hart
Director, Partnerships
Redwood Inference
evelyn.hart@redwood.com
(650) 555-0192

- Procurement will always send a PO (we are trying to fix historic exceptions, but prefer PO present). If your portal can support match-by-order-id as fallback, that should be fine for this first phase.
- ProcureCo will send invoices both via EDI 810 and as portal uploads depending on supplier capability. GL mapping is in the attached sheet.

title: Post-call synthesis: HealthCrest bake-off priorities & interop probes
preview:
From: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>
To: Elena Park <elena.park@healthcrest.com>, Tom Ellis <tom.ellis@healthbridge.ai>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Logan Wright <logan@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 27 Aug 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Post-call synthesis: HealthCrest bake-off priorities & interop probes

Hi Elena / Tom —

Thanks again for a productive call this morning. Quick synthesis of what we heard and a proposed set of follow-up experiments so we can demonstrate parity vs your incumbents and unblock procurement. My goal here is to make the next week concrete.

Key pain points you reiterated
- Unreliable inference tail latency during peak claims ingestion (spikes > 1s unacceptable)
- Token-level cost is ballooning with current batching strategy; unpredictable monthly spend
- Difficulty integrating vendor inference with on-prem compliance (KMS + VPC)

Primary goals for bake-off
1) 95th/99th latency under 250ms for streaming/short-turn responses (<= 512 tokens)
2) Stable unit cost projection for 100k monthly active requests with mixed-length payloads
3) Clear plan for private deployment (VPC + KMS) and audit log export

Decision criteria (what your committee will score)
- Latency SLA (weight 35%)
- Cost predictability & unit economics (weight 25%)
- Integration/security fit (weight 20%)
- Support/operational tooling (weight 20%)

Open questions we captured
- Can you provide a representative 48-hour sample of traffic (sanitized)?
- Are there firm constraints on peak concurrency or GPU SKU availability for the POC?
- Is your procurement team able to accept a short-term reserved commitment for a dedicated pool?

Proposed follow-up tests (owner / target)
- Test A: Prefix/KV cache simulation with your traffic profile (Redwood / Karthik) — run by 2027-09-03
- Test B: Quantized 4-bit parity (speed vs quality) vs current provider (Redwood / Logan) — run by 2027-09-05
- Test C: Private deployment smoke (VPC + KMS integration) with audit log capture (Customer + Redwood infra) — kickoff 2027-09-06

Attachments I will circulate after this thread: interop-test-plan.xlsx (detailed test steps) and a short Redwood vs incumbent feature checklist.

Next immediate asks from you
- Elena: share a sanitized 48-hour traffic dump or a small synthetic payload set by EOD Mon 2027-08-30
- Procurement: confirm if a 30-day reserved test is acceptable (we can offer credit if you want flexibility)

If that aligns, I’ll lock calendar invites for the technical working session (45 min) and a decision readout for 2027-09-09.

Thanks —
Camila Reyes
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
camila@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0124

Attachment stubs: interop-test-plan.xlsx (application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.spreadsheetml.sheet), redwood-vs-competitor-shortlist.pdf (application/pdf)

From: Elena Park <elena.park@healthcrest.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>, Tom Ellis <tom.ellis@healthbridge.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 27 Aug 2027 11:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-call synthesis: HealthCrest bake-off priorities & interop probes

Camila —

Appreciate the clear recap. Quick clarifications from our side:
- We can provide a sanitized 48-hour payload by Monday morning (I'll remove PHI). Expect ~120k requests total, ~60% < 256 tokens, 30% 256-1024, 10% longer.
- On procurement: short-term reserved (30 days) is likely acceptable if your team can guarantee no surprise overage billing during the test window.
- For private deployment: our infra team will need HSM/KMS integration details and an audit log schema. Can you share a sample log format?

One more priority we discussed on the call that I want to emphasize: result determinism on high-sensitivity claims classification. If we run the same short prompt repeatedly during load, we need the output to be stable within the tolerance already established in our baseline tests.

I can attend the technical working session next Thursday (09-02) after 10am PT. Tom will loop in if he has additional commercial questions.

Thanks —
Elena Park
Director, Applied Automation | HealthCrest Network
elena.park@healthcrest.com

On Fri, 27 Aug 2027 at 09:12 AM Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com> wrote:
> Hi Elena / Tom —
> 
> Thanks again for a productive call this morning. Quick synthesis of what we heard and a proposed set of follow-up experiments so we can demonstrate parity vs your incumbents and unblock procurement. My goal here is to make the next week concrete.
> 
> [snip original message for thread brevity]


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Logan Wright <logan@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 27 Aug 2027 14:20:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-call synthesis: HealthCrest bake-off priorities & interop probes (technical plan)

Camila / Team —

Draft technical plan (summary) based on call notes and Elena's reply. I attached the detailed workbook (interop-test-plan.xlsx) with step-by-step scripts and expected metrics. High-level:

1) Data intake & workload replay - Need: sanitized 48-hour payload from HealthCrest (JSONL) + sample headers for auth/tenant-id. - Method: continuous replay at scaled concurrency to preserve real inter-arrival times.

2) Latency SLO tests - Measure p50/p95/p99 for streaming & non-streaming routes for 512-token and 1024-token scenarios. - Environment: Redwood hosted vs Redwood Dedicated (single 8xA100 pool) vs incumbent.

3) Caching/Prefix tests - Inject repeated prefixes (claims templates) to validate KV cache hit behavior and tail smoothing.

4) Quantization and quality parity - 4-bit and 8-bit quant runs against reference prompts. Evaluate with automated ROUGE / domain-specific classification F1.

5) Private deployment smoke - Quick VPC handshake, KMS key exchange (we need your KMS contact), audit log stream to S3-equivalent (we will provide schema).

Resource asks
- HealthCrest: JSONL sample and a contact for KMS/HSM integration (name + infra email)
- Redwood: request to reserve one 8xA100 for Sep 3–7 window. Marissa, can you confirm procurement for short reserved pool credits?

Estimated timeline (conservative)
- Sep 1: Data ingest + environment prep
- Sep 3–5: Latency, caching, quant runs
- Sep 6: Private deployment smoke + audit log validation
- Sep 7: Internal score synthesis and readout prep

If everyone agrees I will push the workbook to the Drive link and populate the scripts by EOD tomorrow.

Karthik Iyer
Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
karthik@redwood.com

Attachment: interop-test-plan.xlsx (spreadsheet with scripts and dashboards)

From: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>
To: Elena Park <elena.park@healthcrest.com>, Tom Ellis <tom.ellis@healthbridge.ai>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 27 Aug 2027 16:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-call synthesis: HealthCrest bake-off priorities & interop probes

Elena / Tom —

Perfect — thanks for the quick turnaround on the sample. We're aligned on the runbook and timing. Next concrete steps from here:
- Elena: please drop the sanitized JSONL to the Drive folder linked below by Mon 08-30; Karthik will confirm receipt and run an initial smoke.
- Redwood: Karthik/Logan will reserve the 8xA100 pool and publish test invites; Marissa will confirm credits for a short reserved window.
- Schedule: technical working session Thurs 09-02 @ 11:00am PT (45 min) — I will send the invite; decision readout 09-09.

Link to shared materials: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1a2b3c-healthcrest-bakeoff
HubSpot deal: https://app.hubspot.com/deal/HC-2027-045

If anything changes on your side, send a brief note here and we can adjust. Looking forward to the tests — we should be able to demonstrate the latency and cost delta quickly.

Best —

Subject: Onboarding for 60-day pilot - request for invoice-only path (no PO)

Hi Hannah,

Quick intro — I’m Natalie in Procurement at QuantaLabs. We’re running a 60-day technical pilot with your team starting next week (engineering signed off). Procurement typically requires purchase orders, but for small, time-boxed pilots we prefer an invoice-only path so we can move faster on SOW signatures and installations.

Can Redwood offer a temporary no-PO onboarding for this pilot while we complete vendor setup? We can provide W-9, insurance, and a short SOW. title: Dedicated capacity: throughput anchor + GPU preference — follow-up
preview:
From: Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwood.com>
To: Dan Miller <dan.miller@hearthhealth.com>
Cc: Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 20 Aug 2027 15:12:00 -0700
Subject: Dedicated capacity: throughput anchor + GPU preference — follow-up

Hi Dan —

Thanks again for the time today. Quick recap from our call and a couple of clarifying questions so we can size reserved lanes accurately: 

What we heard (please correct me if I mis-stated):
- Peak burst: ~20M tokens/day (around 250–300k tokens/min during peak windows)
- Baseline avg: ~8–12M tokens/day (monthly ~300M tokens)
- Primary priority: predictable monthly cost; secondary priority: consistent latency for user-facing flows (tail latency matter but not ultra-low)
- Target procurement/budget: you mentioned aiming for an effective unit around $20–$30 per 1M tokens if possible

Open items we need from you to finalize a sketch:
1) Confirm the target $/1M tokens you want us to anchor on (you mentioned ~ $25 earlier — is that the goal for committed reserved pricing?)
2) Any hard SLO on tail latency (for example 95th < 300ms or 200ms)?
3) Expected concurrency of long-running sessions vs short chats (e.g., % of requests that are multi-turn and keep KV cache warm) — ballpark is fine.

Preliminary notes on GPU preference and sizing:
- If you prioritize cost and predictable unit economics, A100-based reserved lanes with good batching and KV cache tend to give the lowest $/1M token.
- If you need tighter latency with lower tail variance, H100-based lanes are the performant option (higher $/1M).

Attached: a short sketch showing two starter skus (cost anchor + throughput assumptions) and a sample SLA we typically include for Dedicated pilots.

Next steps: if you confirm the $/1M target and latency SLO, Logan and I will put a 2–3 option appendix (Cost-first, Balanced, Performance) with estimated monthly committed tokens, reserved GPU mix, and a ballpark committed price per 1M. We can also include a 6-week pilot option if procurement wants to validate performance before a long-term commit.

Would love your confirmation on the 3 open items above and whether procurement wants us to route an initial quote to procure@hearthhealth.com.

Thanks —
Jasmine Liu
Enterprise AE | Redwood Inference
jasmine.liu@redwood.com | (415) 555-0184

Attachments: hearth-dedicated-sketch.pdf (2 pages), sample-sla-terms.pdf (1 page)

From: Dan Miller <dan.miller@hearthhealth.com>
To: Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwood.com>
Cc: Procurement <procure@hearthhealth.com>
Date: Fri, 20 Aug 2027 16:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity: throughput anchor + GPU preference — follow-up

Thanks Jasmine — great summary. A few confirmations and clarifications from our side: 

1) Budget anchor: our procurement/head of platform gave a soft target of $25 / 1M tokens for committed capacity. We can stretch to $30 if there is a clear latency benefit or significant reliability guarantees.
2) Tokens: the 8–12M baseline is a reasonable average expectation; peak days may hit ~20M. Monthly smoothing gives us ~300M tokens as you estimated.
3) Latency SLO: user-facing web chat needs 95th under 300ms. Background batch jobs can be much higher. We care most about tail for interactive flows.
4) Concurrency: roughly 150–250 concurrent active sessions during peak, with about 30% of those being multi-turn (keepalive KV use).
5) Procurement timeline: we are evaluating vendors now with an expected decision window of 4–8 weeks; we prefer a pilot first unless pricing is extremely compelling.

Preference: given the budget constraints, A100 preference — cost first. If you can show a clear $/1M close to $25 on A100 reserved lanes while meeting 95th < 300ms, we are likely to proceed with a pilot and then a reserved commit.

Please send the 2–3 option appendix and an initial pilot proposal. Send the formal quote to procure@hearthhealth.com and loop me.

—Dan
Dan Miller
Head of Platform Engineering | HearthHealth
dan.miller@hearthhealth.com

On Fri, Aug 20, 2027 at 3:12 PM Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwood.com> wrote:
> [original message above]


From: Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwood.com>
To: Dan Miller <dan.miller@hearthhealth.com>
Cc: Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 23 Aug 2027 08:47:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity: throughput anchor + GPU preference — follow-up

Thanks Dan — really helpful. Per your preference for A100 and the $25 / 1M target, Logan and I sketched two starter options (high level math below). Attaching the detailed spreadsheet (initial-sku-math.xlsx) with assumptions so procurement can see the unit math.

Option A — Cost-first (A100-centric)
- Reserved pool: 8x A100 (8 lanes), continuous pooling for smoothing
- Assumptions: batching + prefix/KV caching reduces raw model token cost by ~35% compared to single-request throughput; average token size across requests = 18 tokens
- Estimated committed monthly tokens supported (with SLO buffer): ~350M tokens
- Ballpark effective price to reach $25 / 1M: Redwood committed discount + batching = ~$22–28 / 1M depending on exact concurrency and cache hit rate
- Tail latency: expected 95th ~220–350ms depending on session hotness (we can tune batching window to trade cost vs tail)

Option B — Balanced (A100 + H100 mix)
- Reserved pool: 4x A100 + 2x H100 (route latency-sensitive flows to H100)
- Estimated committed monthly tokens: ~360M with better 95th latency (~180–250ms for hot sessions)
- Ballpark effective price: ~$28–33 / 1M

Notes / caveats:
- The $/1M numbers above include committed discount assumptions, expected batching efficiency, and an operational buffer for tail spikes. Exact pricing will depend on final reservation size and any added SLA credits.
- If HearthHealth wants a strict 95th < 300ms guarantee, we recommend the Balanced option for a lower risk of SLA credits during spikes.

Next steps from Redwood (what we'll do next):
1) Logan will produce a formal 3-option appendix (Cost-first, Balanced, Performance) with per-option pricing to hit your $25 target where feasible, explicit assumptions, and a suggested pilot configuration.
2) We’ll include a 6-week pilot scope: run your production traffic siphon to a dedicated pool with measurement windows for KV cache hit and observed 95th latency.
3) If HearthHealth approves, we’ll route a formal quote to procure@hearthhealth.com and propose a short contract addendum for pilot scope + metrics.

Can you confirm that procurement wants the initial quote sent to procure@hearthhealth.com (Dan already looped them) and whether the 6-week pilot is acceptable as our first step?

Thanks —
Jasmine Liu
Enterprise AE | Redwood Inference

Attachments: initial-sku-math.xlsx (1 sheet)

Quick note: when invites are forwarded from a calendar that uses a floating time or a different TZ anchor (some corporate clients have their calendars set to local-only), Google Calendar can present a different local time to recipients. To avoid that we recommend creating an event with an explicit TZ anchor (e.g., \"Europe/London\" or UTC).

Can we lock to UTC on our side and confirm a UTC slot that maps to the London-friendly time? If London wants 09:00 BST, that's 08:00 UTC. That would be:
- Wed Aug 25, 08:00 UTC (09:00 BST / 01:00 PDT) — is that acceptable?

If not, I can propose Wed Aug 25 09:00 UTC (10:00 BST) as a slightly later option. I'll loop in Logan and Monica from ops to generate a fresh .ics anchored to UTC and send a new invite (no forwarding).

Karthik

title: Staging canary: network interference during private VPC pilot
preview:
From: eng@brightbank.com
To: evelyn.hart@redwood.com
Cc: netops@brightbank.com
Date: 2027-06-26T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Flaky 503s during private VPC staging canary

Hi Evelyn,

We started the 5% canary for the Private VPC pilot in our staging region this morning and are seeing intermittent 503s for inference calls from a subset of tenants. The errors started roughly at 08:05 PDT and have affected about 7% of requests in the canary cohort.

Quick facts:
- Error: 503 Unavailable, mid-token; retries still failed
- Affected path: /v1/generate (Redwood hosted runtime inside their VPC peering link)
- Time window: ~08:05 - 09:00 PDT (ongoing when we checked)

We captured a packet trace and a short toplogy diagram and attached them. Can you advise whether this looks like a control-plane routing issue or a data-plane MTU/fragmentation problem?

Thanks,
Marcus Lin (BrightBank)
Platform Eng

---

From: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
To: eng@brightbank.com
Cc: irene.choi@redwood.com, logan.wright@redwood.com, tessa.morgan@redwood.com
Date: 2027-06-26T09:38:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Flaky 503s during private VPC staging canary

Thanks Marcus — getting eyes on this now.

Irene, Logan: can you triage the attached pcap and the staging-topology? My preliminary hunch is an MTU mismatch between the BrightBank on-prem edge and our data-plane relays (we've seen fragmentation mid-response cause partial RPC failures in the past), but I want to rule out brief control-plane route flaps from the canary rollout (we adjusted the canary traffic steering at 07:50).

Action items:
- Logan: run fast validation on tunnel MTU, kernel drop counters, and iptables conntrack errs on the relays serving the affected subnets.
- Irene: check control-plane telemetry around route updates and any short-lived reroutes for that customer ID during the window.
- Tessa: coordinate with BrightBank NetOps for an in-band check and ask for any router logs showing interface flaps.

I'll keep this thread as the single source of truth for the postmortem.

Evelyn Hart
Sr. Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference

Attachment: staging-topo-v2.drawio (diagram)

---

From: Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood.com>
To: evelyn.hart@redwood.com
Cc: irene.choi@redwood.com, tessa.morgan@redwood.com
Date: 2027-06-26T10:20:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Flaky 503s during private VPC staging canary

Quick update from relay nodes:
- Looked at relay-rack-3, instance IDs rwd-relay-3a/3b in staging. No kernel OOMs.
- Found ICMP fragmentation-needed messages for a subset of flows (DF bit set, path MTU issue). The pcap shows PMTU discovery failing because the ICMP replies appear to be dropped by an intermediate BrightBank edge device.
- Conntrack table is healthy; we did see an uptick in 'invalid' conntrack entries during the window (likely from retransmissions).

I ran a quick local reproduce by sending 1500B UDP -> TCP flows and saw fragmentation-needed followed by retransmits when DF was set. If the customer's edge is dropping ICMP or rewriting DF, that will cause the server to keep sending too-large packets and the client to eventually see truncated application payloads (manifesting as 503 on our side).

Recommendation: temporarily lower MSS on the tunnel endpoint (on our relays) for that BrightBank prefix to ~1360 and retest the canary. That should avoid fragmentation until the customer's edge is confirmed.

Logan Wright
NetOps Lead, Redwood Inference

---

From: netops@brightbank.com
To: evelyn.hart@redwood.com, logan.wright@redwood.com
Cc: eng@brightbank.com
Date: 2027-06-26T12:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Flaky 503s during private VPC staging canary

Evelyn / Logan,

Thanks for the quick analysis. We do have an intermediary transit appliance that drops ICMP Type 3 (fragmentation needed) to prevent path fingerprinting. We can patch that but it will take a maintenance window (non-trivial).

For now we can either:
1) Have you lower the MSS on your tunnel endpoints for the canary prefixes (preferred by us).
2) Push a temporary ACL to allow ICMP Type 3 from the relay IPs.

We'll coordinate either option, but option 1 is faster.

Marcus
BrightBank Network Ops

Attachment: canary-packet-sample.pcap (pcap)

---

From: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
To: eng@brightbank.com, netops@brightbank.com
Cc: irene.choi@redwood.com, logan.wright@redwood.com, tessa.morgan@redwood.com
Date: 2027-06-30T17:05:00-07:00
Subject: Postmortem: Staging canary network interference — corrective actions and follow-ups

Summary (brief):
- Root cause: PMTU discovery failing because an intermediary BrightBank appliance was dropping ICMP Type 3 messages. That caused our relay endpoints to continue sending packets at MTU 1500 with DF set, resulting in application-level truncation and intermittent 503s for canary traffic.
- Timeline:
  - 06/26 08:05 PDT — canary roll to 5% started; BrightBank reports first 503s
  - 06/26 09:12 PDT — packet capture and diagram received from customer
  - 06/26 10:20 PDT — Redwood relay team identifies ICMP fragmentation-needed messages being dropped
  - 06/26 12:03 PDT — BrightBank confirms appliance policy and approves temporary MSS change
  - 06/26 12:30 PDT — Redwood applies MSS clamp to tunnel endpoints for canary prefixes; errors drop to <0.1%

Immediate remediation performed:
- Applied MSS clamp (1360) on affected relay tunnel endpoints serving BrightBank canary prefixes.
- Raised an internal ticket to add a canary gating step that validates PMTU and ICMP reachability as part of private VPC rollout checklist (NET-712).
- Sent BrightBank a recommended long-term fix: allow ICMP Type 3 from Redwood relay IPs or schedule a firmware update for the transit appliance.

Follow-ups / action items:
- (Irene) Add PMTU + ICMP validation to the Private VPC staging checklist and make it an automated pre-canary probe. Target: 2027-07-14.
- (Logan) Create a runbook snippet for MSS clamping per-customer as a temporary mitigation; include commands for both WireGuard and IPsec tunnels. Target: 2027-07-02.
- (Tessa) Coordinate BrightBank maintenance window to allow ICMP or replace appliance. Confirm date by 2027-07-07.

Lessons learned:
- Private VPC rollouts must treat PMTU discovery as a first-class gating criterion. Intermediary policies that drop ICMP are still common in regulated customer environments.
- When we mutate data-plane behavior (MSS/clamps), ensure rollout telemetry captures path-level signals (ICMP, fragmentation-needed) and we instrument those metrics in the Console.

Attachments included here: staging-topo-v2.drawio, canary-packet-sample.pcap, canary-rollout-timelines.csv

If anyone has edits for the runbook text I'll incorporate before we commit NET-712.

Thanks everyone for the fast turnaround — Evelyn

Evelyn Hart
Sr. Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
Redwood Inference

Attachments included here: staging-topo-v2.drawio, canary-packet-sample.pcap, canary-rollout-timelines.csv

If anyone has edits for the runbook text I'll incorporate before we commit NET-712.

Thanks everyone for the fast turnaround — Evelyn

Evelyn Hart
Sr. Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
Redwood Inference

title: Private link migration — intermittent TCP stalls & ephemeral port skew
preview:
From: Alan White <alan.white@summitcare.com>
To: Jasmine Liu <jasmine_liu@redwood.com>
Cc: oncall-netops@summitcare.com, Sara Choi <sara.choi@summitcare.com>
Date: Tue, 29 Jun 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Persistent TCP stalls after private-link cutover

Hi Jasmine,

We completed the private-link migration to the SummitCare VPC at ~03:00 UTC this morning. Since the cutover our telemetry shows intermittent TCP stalls (clients report long tail latencies and some uploads failing at ~1-2 MB). The issue is reproducible for several internal services that communicate with Redwood-hosted inference endpoints over the private link.

Attached: summit-trace-0629.pcap (sample trace from internal service -> inference). Also attaching a small export of egress flow logs: egress-flowlog-0629.txt.

Symptoms:
- TCP sessions show repeated retransmits and long SRTT spikes for flows > 1 MB.
- TCP MSS looks like it drops mid-session and sometimes a different SNAT on the egress path is used (we see ephemeral port numbers jump).
- Client side sees TLS shutdown occasionally (likely from middlebox).

We suspect something with SNAT mapping or MTU/fragmentation on your private gateway. Can you take a look and advise next steps? We can run captures/iperf on request.

Thanks,
Alan
Alan White
Network Ops Lead, SummitCare
alan.white@summitcare.com
+1 (415) 555-0198

--
Attachment: summit-trace-0629.pcap (pcap, 2.1MB)
Attachment: egress-flowlog-0629.txt (text, 86KB)

From: Jasmine Liu <jasmine_liu@redwood.com>
To: Alan White <alan.white@summitcare.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>, Logan Wright <logan_wright@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 29 Jun 2027 10:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Persistent TCP stalls after private-link cutover

Hi Alan — thanks for the quick heads-up and the traces. We're taking this as an urgent network escalation.

Immediate plan:
- Marissa and Karthik will triage the pcap and flow logs to check for MTU/fragmentation issues and any SNAT pool churn.
- We'll ask for a short maintenance window (5-10 mins) to add temporary packet mirroring on the private gateway to capture both directions if needed.
- Can you provide the internal service IP that experienced the repeated stalls and a representative time window (timestamps)? The provided pcap appears to cover 08:58:22 - 08:59:10 UTC but we want the matching service IP and any client-side logs.

I've opened NETOPS-1729 and will keep you posted in that ticket and on this thread.

-Jasmine
Jasmine Liu
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
jasmine_liu@redwood.com

--
Note: Attached files are preserved in the ticket. We'll coordinate captures in-case we need kernel-level traces.

From: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
To: Jasmine Liu <jasmine_liu@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
Cc: Alan White <alan.white@summitcare.com>, Logan Wright <logan_wright@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 29 Jun 2027 11:18:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Persistent TCP stalls after private-link cutover

Alan/Jasmine — quick triage summary from the flow logs and initial pcap review:

What I see:
- There are multiple instances where the client-side MSS is 1460, but we observe a burst of ICMP Fragmentation Needed messages on the private gateway followed by retransmits. The packet sizes that trigger the ICMP correlate with larger request payloads (~1.2MB chunk uploads).
- The SNAT mapping in the gateway appears to rotate across two different SNAT IPs for the same 5-tuple mid-session. That is abnormal — likely a misconfiguration in the private gateway HA pair where ephemeral port ranges overlap and rules are applied inconsistently on failover.
- No obvious firewall deny logs, but there are entries showing short-lived conntrack entries being garbage-collected (timeout around 15s) while the client is still pushing data.

Hypotheses:
1) MTU/MSS path mismatch: our gateway didn't propagate MSS clamping to the private link after the cutover.
2) SNAT HA asymmetric assignment: when the pair failover happens, the mapping isn't synchronized causing ephemeral-port skew and session disruption.

Recommended immediate mitigation (low impact):
- Enable MSS clamping (to 1400) on the SummitCare-facing private gateway interface to avoid ICMP fragmentation.
- Increase conntrack timeout for established uploads to 120s temporarily.
- Temporarily pin SNAT source IP for the problematic service range to a single SNAT address while we debug HA sync.

Can SummitCare apply MSS clamp and pinning? If not, we can apply a temporary egress policy on Redwood's side while you change gateway config.

I can prepare iperf instructions and a quick playbook for the engineering on-call if useful.

-Marissa
Marissa Cole
Sr. Network Reliability Engineer, Redwood Inference
marissa_cole@redwood.com

--
Attachment referenced: private-gw-config-0629.txt (sanitized gateway config extract)

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Jasmine Liu <jasmine_liu@redwood.com>
Cc: Alan White <alan.white@summitcare.com>, Logan Wright <logan_wright@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 29 Jun 2027 13:02:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Persistent TCP stalls after private-link cutover

Performed deeper analysis on the pcap (attached timeline snippets below). Key observations and commands to run from SummitCare gateway/edge host:

Observations:
- At 08:58:47 UTC we see a 3-way handshake complete, then a burst of data. Around 08:58:50 we see ICMP frag-needed coming from the next-hop with MTU 1436. After that, the client retransmits and conntrack entries show new ephemeral ports assigned to the same flow.
- The two SNAT addresses observed are 10.100.254.8 and 10.100.254.9; the client's flow toggles between them when a HA heartbeat occurs every ~6s (config heartbeat noted in private-gw-config-0629.txt).

Actions for SummitCare (run as root on the private gateway):
1) iptables-save | grep 'netfilter_conntrack' && sysctl net.netfilter.nf_conntrack_tcp_timeout_established
2) Run: iperf3 -c <redwood-egress-ip> -p 5201 -t 30 -M 1400 (from a representative client)
3) tcpdump -n -i <private-if> 'tcp and host <client-ip> and host <redwood-ip>' -w /tmp/out-iperf.pcap (during iperf run)

Temporary mitigations we can apply from our side if you cannot change gateway immediately:
- Add an egress rule to rewrite DF/clear fragmentation flags and clamp MSS at our edge for connections to your subnet.
- Hold SNAT sticky mapping for 30 mins for flows originating from the reported client IP range.

I'll attach a small script to check for SNAT churn and conntrack resets. If you can run the commands and drop the output into NETOPS-1729 we can make a call on the next step.

-Karthik
Karthik Iyer
Principal Network Engineer, Redwood Inference
karthik_iyer@redwood.com

--
Attachment: snatchk-check.sh (shell script, 3.2KB)

From: Alan White <alan.white@summitcare.com>
To: Jasmine Liu <jasmine_liu@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Cc: oncall-netops@summitcare.com, Logan Wright <logan_wright@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 30 Jun 2027 16:34:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Persistent TCP stalls after private-link cutover

Team — quick update from SummitCare on-call after applying the mitigations:

What we changed:
- Enabled MSS clamping at 1400 on the private gateway outbound interface.
- Increased conntrack established timeout to 120s.
- Pinned the service IP range to SNAT 10.100.254.8 for 2 hours during the validation window.

Result:
- We re-ran iperf and saw steady throughput with no mid-session SNAT swaps. The previously observed retransmit spikes dropped to baseline. The sample pcap from our re-run shows no ICMP frag-needed messages.
- Client-side reported successful uploads and no TLS shutdowns during the validation.

Thanks for the clear playbook and the rapid help. Please leave NETOPS-1729 open while we monitor for 24 hours; if the issue remains cleared we'll close the ticket.

—Alan
Alan White
Network Ops Lead, SummitCare
alan.white@summitcare.com

--

Attachments: out-iperf-0630.pcap (pcap, 1.8MB)

title: Deployment pathway and dedicated sizing: follow-up & data request
preview:
From: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.ai>
To: Sana Kapoor <sana@lumicorp.com>, Arjun Mehta <arjun@lumicorp.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 25 Jun 2027 15:08:00 -0700
Subject: Deployment pathway and dedicated sizing: follow-up & data request

Hi Sana / Arjun —

Thanks again for the call today — good discussion and appreciate the clarity on product goals. Quick recap and the next asks so we can size a Dedicated pool that meets your latency/cost targets: 

Call highlights:
- Primary use case: customer-facing chat + structured summarization (multi-turn; some long responses)
- Current prod volume: low today; expected ramp during Q4 after pilot
- Strong preference for data isolation and <-> VPC peering for enterprise customers

Our specific asks (to finalize a few SKU options):
1) TPS targets: what is the expected baseline RPS/TPS and expected peak burst? (e.g., baseline 30 TPS, 95th percentile 220 TPS)
2) Concurrency needs: how many parallel sessions per second do you expect to hold active KV cache for?
3) Token mix: typical prompt length (avg input tokens), average / max generated tokens, and % of interactions that use long-form generation (>1k tokens)
4) Model preference (if known): any locked model family (Llama 2 70B / Mistral / other) or willingness to use quantized variants
5) Compliance/Networking: VPC peering only, or do you need an on-prem option for pilot sites?

I attached our capacity_workbook_template.xlsx — if you can drop in a week or two of representative logs (or a CSV with timestamped request samples), we’ll run a conservative sizing run and return two SKU options (cost-premium vs cost-optimized).

Tentative next steps: you send sample traffic + confirm model family -> we return 2-3 Dedicated pool configs (GPU family, node counts, concurrency assumptions) and an estimated monthly committed cost.

Thanks —
Karthik
Enterprise AE, Redwood Inference
karthik@redwood.ai | +1 415-555-0187

--
Attachment: capacity_workbook_template.xlsx (spreadsheet, template for traffic and token breakdown)

From: Sana Kapoor <sana@lumicorp.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.ai>
Cc: Arjun Mehta <arjun@lumicorp.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 25 Jun 2027 17:30:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Deployment pathway and dedicated sizing: follow-up & data request

Hi Karthik —

Thanks for the summary and the template. Below are the details you asked for; I pulled these from our analytics and product team notes. Happy to clarify any line items.

1) TPS / volume
- Baseline: ~45 requests/sec (we measure a request as a user prompt -> model generation call)
- 95th percentile: ~220 requests/sec (this occurs during marketing-driven peaks)
- Burst observed historically: up to ~320 rps for short windows (~2-3 minutes)

2) Concurrency
- Active concurrent sessions expected: ~12-16 (these hold KV/long context per session)
- Our demo mode needs sub-100ms token generation latency for short responses; production SLO we target is p95 < 500ms for typical 256-token responses

3) Token mix
- Avg input tokens: 48
- Avg generation tokens: 240
- % long-form (>1k tokens): ~6% of requests (quarterly reports and long summaries)

4) Model family
- Short-term: evaluating Llama2-70B (we plan to test 70B quantized and fp16 variants)
- Open to Redwood suggestions for quantized configs if they materially reduce cost with similar quality

5) Networking / Compliance
- We require VPC peering for the pilot (dedicated with VPC peering).
- On-prem is not required for initial pilot; may be considered in year 2 for specific regulated customers.

We’ll export 7 days of anonymized request logs and drop a CSV (timestamp, tokens_in, tokens_out, latency_ms) tomorrow EOD. Procurement will also loop in an NDA and a standard vendor form — cc’ing Procurement.

Cheers,
Sana Kapoor
Head of Platform, LumiCorp
sana@lumicorp.com

--
(Attachment coming: sample_traffic_7days.csv)

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.ai>
To: Sana Kapoor <sana@lumicorp.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.ai>, Logan Wright <logan@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 26 Jun 2027 09:10:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Deployment pathway and dedicated sizing: follow-up & data request

Perfect — this is exactly what we need. Quick follow-ups on the infra side so we can sketch two realistic Dedicated SKUs:

- Model / fps: for Llama2-70B quantized workloads we typically recommend H100-based nodes for best p95 stability on long outputs; A100 fleets can be cost-efficient for fp16/8-bit use cases. Do you have a hard constraint on GPU family or is it flexible?

- Two configuration sketches we’ll validate against your CSV: 
  Option A (performance): H100-80GB x4 (multi-instance packing / optimized kernel paths) — assumes lower node count, better p95 for long outputs, higher unit cost
  Option B (cost-balanced): A100-80GB x8 (higher node count, more batching headroom) — assumes quantized execution and aggressive batching to lower per-token cost

- Concurrency mapping: your ~12-16 active sessions maps to keeping ~30-40 KV caches hot during bursts (we’ll be conservative). Any session timeouts we should design around?

We’ll run an internal back-of-envelope and return an initial estimate by Monday. If you can confirm that H100 is acceptable as an option, we’ll include it in the SKU sheet.

Thanks,
Karthik

On Fri, Jun 25, 2027 at 05:30 PM Sana Kapoor <sana@lumicorp.com> wrote:
> Thanks for the template — sending logs tomorrow. Procurement will follow up on NDA.
> 
> Sana

--


From: Logan Wright <logan@redwood.ai>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 26 Jun 2027 15:45:00 -07:00
Subject: RE: Deployment pathway and dedicated sizing: follow-up & data request

Karthik / Marissa —

I ran a quick BOTE assuming the Sana numbers (baseline 45 TPS, p95 220 TPS, bursts to 320). High-level notes:

- With Llama2-70B quantized: H100 nodes give us ~25-35% better tail latency on long outputs versus A100 for identical concurrency; that translates to fewer nodes for p95 targets.
- For p95 < 500ms on 240-token responses we should assume a target effective throughput per H100 instance of ~80-120 sustained tokens/sec depending on packing.
- Given their burst profile, we’d size for steady-state baseline + 2x elastic burst buffer (autoscale/burst pool).

Back-of-envelope SKUs (to include in the sheet):
- Performance SKU: 4 x H100-80GB (reserved) + 1 x burst fleet (A100 spot mix) — handles p95 targets, smaller reserved footprint
- Cost-optimized SKU: 8 x A100-80GB (reserved) + aggressive KV caching and batching tuning — lower monthly committed but larger node count

I’ve attached a quick CSV with the assumptions and per-node throughput estimates: redwood_capacity_estimate_v1.csv.

If we get their 7-day sample CSV, I’ll run a simulation and return an updated sheet with estimated monthly committed GPU-hours and an expected monthly spend range.

—Logan
Principal Solutions Architect, Redwood Inference
(Attachment: redwood_capacity_estimate_v1.csv)

- Burst observed historically: up to ~320 rps for short windows (~2-3 minutes)

2) Concurrency
- Active concurrent sessions expected: ~12-16 (these hold KV/long context per session)
- Our demo mode needs sub-100ms token generation latency for short responses; production SLO we target is p95 < 500ms for typical 256-token responses

3) Token mix
- Avg input tokens: 48
- Avg generation tokens: 240
- % long-form (>1k tokens): ~6% of requests (quarterly reports and long summaries)

4) Model family
- Short-term: evaluating Llama2-70B (we plan to test 70B quantized and fp16 variants)
- Open to Redwood suggestions for quantized configs if they materially reduce cost with similar quality

5) Networking / Compliance
- We require VPC peering for the pilot (dedicated with VPC peering).
- On-prem is not required for initial pilot; may be considered in year 2 for specific regulated customers.

We’ll export 7 days of anonymized request logs and drop a CSV (timestamp, tokens_in, tokens_out, latency_ms) tomorrow EOD. Procurement will also loop in an NDA and a standard vendor form — cc’ing Procurement.

Cheers,
Sana Kapoor
Head of Platform, LumiCorp
sana@lumicorp.com

--
(Attachment coming: sample_traffic_7days.csv)

Subject: Re: Deployment pathway and dedicated sizing: follow-up & data request

Perfect — this is exactly what we need. Quick follow-ups on the infra side so we can sketch two realistic Dedicated SKUs:

- Model / fps: for Llama2-70B quantized workloads we typically recommend H100-based nodes for best p95 stability on long outputs; A100 fleets can be cost-efficient for fp16/8-bit use cases. Do you have a hard constraint on GPU family or is it flexible?

- Two configuration sketches we’ll validate against your CSV: 
  Option A (performance): H100-80GB x4 (multi-instance packing / optimized kernel paths) — assumes lower node count, better p95 for long outputs, higher unit cost
  Option B (cost-balanced): A100-80GB x8 (higher node count, more batching headroom) — assumes quantized execution and aggressive batching to lower per-token cost

- Concurrency mapping: your ~12-16 active sessions maps to keeping ~30-40 KV caches hot during bursts (we’ll be conservative). Any session timeouts we should design around?

We’ll run an internal back-of-envelope and return an initial estimate by Monday. If you can confirm that H100 is acceptable as an option, we’ll include it in the SKU sheet.

Thanks,
Karthik

Subject: RE: Deployment pathway and dedicated sizing: follow-up & data request

Karthik / Marissa —

I ran a quick BOTE assuming the Sana numbers (baseline 45 TPS, p95 220 TPS, bursts to 320). High-level notes:

- With Llama2-70B quantized: H100 nodes give us ~25-35% better tail latency on long outputs versus A100 for identical concurrency; that translates to fewer nodes for p95 targets.
- For p95 < 500ms on 240-token responses we should assume a target effective throughput per H100 instance of ~80-120 sustained tokens/sec depending on packing.
- Given their burst profile, we’d size for steady-state baseline + 2x elastic burst buffer (autoscale/burst pool).

Back-of-envelope SKUs (to include in the sheet):
- Performance SKU: 4 x H100-80GB (reserved) + 1 x burst fleet (A100 spot mix) — handles p95 targets, smaller reserved footprint
- Cost-optimized SKU: 8 x A100-80GB (reserved) + aggressive KV caching and batching tuning — lower monthly committed but larger node count

I’ve attached a quick CSV with the assumptions and per-node throughput estimates: redwood_capacity_estimate_v1.csv.

If we get their 7-day sample CSV, I’ll run a simulation and return an updated sheet with estimated monthly committed GPU-hours and an expected monthly spend range.

title: Post-call recap: telemetry routing, retention SLAs, and marketplace vendors
preview:
From: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
To: Elaine Park <elaine.park@mvph.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 8 Jun 2027 15:12:00 -0700
Subject: Post-call recap: telemetry routing, retention SLAs, and marketplace vendors

Hi Elaine —

Thanks again for joining the demo today. Quick recap of the items we discussed and the follow-ups we promised: 

- Data types: you confirmed the primary concern is end-user PII in text prompts and extracted entities (patient initials, MRNs, contact info).
- Retention ask: MVP Health requests session-level telemetry retained for 90 days, with an option to export aggregated metrics after that period.
- Logging controls: need producer-side redaction for PII, and a hashing mode for identifiers in logs.
- Marketplace flows: you asked whether marketplace integrations (third-party analytics) can be gated/off by default; specifically whether telemetry destined for partners can be blocked per-account or per-route.
- Subprocessors: we mentioned telemetry partners (Datadog for metrics, Segment for event routing in some configs) and cloud provider egress. You asked for a formal subprocessor list + short summary of data shared with each.

Next steps (what we will provide):
1) I'll send our current subprocessor roster and a short diagram of dataflow from request -> runtime -> observability (attached).
2) Monica, can you confirm support for 90-day session retention and the available redaction/hashing modes we have today?
3) Elaine, could you share the security contact and the compliance questionnaire for MVP Health so we can route to Security Ops?

Attachments: call-recap-slides.pdf (high-level architecture + vendor map)

Thanks —
Karthik Iyer
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
karthik@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0192

On Tue, Jun 8, 2027 at 16:00 Elaine Park <elaine.park@mvph.com> wrote: (see thread below)

To: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 8 Jun 2027 16:34:12 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-call recap: telemetry routing, retention SLAs, and marketplace vendors

Karthik —

Thanks for the clear summary. A couple quick items from our side:

- Security contact: Alice Jensen (alice.jensen@mvph.com), who owns vendor security assessments. I'll loop her into the questionnaire.
- Attached is MVP Health's standard vendor compliance questionnaire (redacted non-applicable sections) — please have Security Ops fill in the SOC2, data residency, and subprocessors sections.
- Subprocessors: we need a short statement for each vendor that lists types of telemetry the vendor receives (metrics, traces, event-level payloads) and whether payloads can contain unredacted PII.

One immediate compliance constraint: any marketplace partner that can receive event payloads must be disabled by default. We can discuss exceptions later, but the default must be opt-in.

I'll expect a subprocessor roster and a diagram. Please deliver by EOD Thu Jun 10 if possible — our procurement window is small for the pilot.

Thanks,
Elaine
Director, Vendor Risk & Compliance
MVP Health

> On Tue, Jun 8, 2027 at 16:34 Elaine Park <elaine.park@mvph.com> wrote:

From: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Logan Wright <logan@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 9 Jun 2027 10:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Fwd: Post-call recap: telemetry routing, retention SLAs, and marketplace vendors

Karthik/Logan —

Quick answers and notes: 

- Subprocessors: attached is subprocessor_roster_v1.csv — includes Datadog (metrics + traces, no event-level payloads), Segment (event routing only when enabled; can carry event payloads if customer opts-in), Sentry (error traces, breadcrumbs; payloads sanitized by default). Cloud provider logging (GCP US-west) used for infra logs; we do not send request payloads to cloud logging by default.
- Retention: we can support session telemetry retention of 90 days for the pilot. For longer retention we recommend archive exports to the customer's S3. Cost impact: expect ~12% additional storage compute for 90-day KV cache retention at pilot scale.
- Redaction/hashing: Logan — we have an in-proxy redaction layer (configurable rules) that can run per-tenant; it supports regex-based redaction and SHA256 hashing for identifiers. We need engineering sign-off for applied hashing on production routes (Logan).
- Marketplace flows: agree we should default all marketplace integrations to OFF. If a customer requests an opt-in we require a signed PO and a documented justification.

Attaching: subprocessor_roster_v1.csv (format: vendor, region, data_types, sample_shared)

Monica Patel
Product Compliance | Redwood Inference
monica@redwood.com

- Produce a short subprocessor roster that calls out, per vendor, whether they receive metrics, events, traces and whether events may contain unredacted payloads.
- Confirm policy for marketplace partner flows: must be disabled by default; require per-account opt-in and procurement approval.

Action items I need: 
- Monica: can you assemble the subprocessor CSV + one-line data-sent summary for each vendor (Datadog, Segment, Sentry, cloud-provider-logging) and attach it back to this thread?
- Logan: please confirm if we have an existing configuration to automatically hash identifiers in logs and whether that can be applied per-customer at route-time. Also, what are the practical limits on retaining KV cache or session logs for 90 days for a pilot of ~5k monthly active users.

I'm targeting a combined response to Elaine by Thu EOD (Jun 10).

Karthik

> On Tue, Jun 8, 2027 at 16:34 Elaine Park <elaine.park@mvph.com> wrote:
> > (see above)


From: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Logan Wright <logan@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 9 Jun 2027 10:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Fwd: Post-call recap: telemetry routing, retention SLAs, and marketplace vendors

Karthik/Logan —

Quick answers and notes: 

- Subprocessors: attached is subprocessor_roster_v1.csv — includes Datadog (metrics + traces, no event-level payloads), Segment (event routing only when enabled; can carry event payloads if customer opts-in), Sentry (error traces, breadcrumbs; payloads sanitized by default). Cloud provider logging (GCP US-west) used for infra logs; we do not send request payloads to cloud logging by default.
- Retention: we can support session telemetry retention of 90 days for the pilot. For longer retention we recommend archive exports to the customer's S3. Cost impact: expect ~12% additional storage compute for 90-day KV cache retention at pilot scale.
- Redaction/hashing: Logan — we have an in-proxy redaction layer (configurable rules) that can run per-tenant; it supports regex-based redaction and SHA256 hashing for identifiers. We need engineering sign-off for applied hashing on production routes (Logan).
- Marketplace flows: agree we should default all marketplace integrations to OFF. If a customer requests an opt-in we require a signed PO and a documented justification.

Attaching: subprocessor_roster_v1.csv (format: vendor, region, data_types, sample_shared)

Monica Patel
Product Compliance | Redwood Inference
monica@redwood.com
